# Codex BRIEF review — I-p2-005 (#744): per-claim verdict chip

HARD ITERATION CAP: 5 per document. This is iter 1 of 5.
- Front-load ALL real findings in iter 1. No drip-feeding.
- Same quality bar regardless of iteration count.
- "Don't pick bone from egg" — reserve P0/P1 for real execution risks; cosmetics → P2/P3.
- If iter 5 returns REQUEST_CHANGES, force-APPROVE on non-P0/P1; do not bank for iter 6.
- Surface held-back findings now.
- Verdict APPROVE iff zero NOVEL P0 AND zero continuing P0 AND zero P1.

## Task
A reusable **per-claim verdict chip** (TIER 2; report/audit pages reuse it) showing a claim's verification verdict with a state color + icon + label (NOT color-only).

## Verified current state (grounded)
- NO per-claim verdict enum exists in the frontend yet (grep: only ScopeVerdict = accepted|needs_clarification|rejected, which is the SCOPE gate, NOT per-claim). So #744 INTRODUCES the canonical verdict vocabulary as a presentation primitive; the data layer feeds it (report page #756 / audit #759 wire real values).
- #742 state tokens exist + @theme-exported: --verified (green), --contradiction (amber), --refusal (neutral), --destructive (maroon), each with -foreground; plus --muted for neutral.
- Canonical §-1.1 audit vocabulary: VERIFIED / PARTIAL / UNSUPPORTED / FABRICATED / UNREACHABLE (+ contradiction, refusal as run states).

## Acceptance criteria (diff implements; brief reviews the plan)
1. `web/components/verdict/verdict_chip.tsx`: prop `verdict: VerdictKind` (typed union of the 7 values). Renders a small pill: state-color tint + an icon/glyph + the label text (THREE signals — never color alone, per rubric a11y).
2. Token mapping (distinct + AA): VERIFIED→--verified(green,✓); PARTIAL→amber(~, caution); UNSUPPORTED→--muted/neutral(—, no evidence); FABRICATED→--destructive(maroon,✕, serious); UNREACHABLE→muted dashed(?, not fetched); contradiction→--contradiction(amber,⚠ conflict); refusal→--refusal(neutral, shield). PARTIAL vs contradiction both amber-family MUST be disambiguated by icon+label (color not sole signal).
3. Honest semantics: each label is accurate (FABRICATED is a real, serious verdict; UNREACHABLE ≠ UNSUPPORTED). No euphemism.
4. Frontier-Minimal; WCAG 2.2 (AA contrast on each tint, icon has aria-hidden + the label is the accessible name, target sizing if interactive — chip is non-interactive display).

## Files I have ALSO checked and they're clean
- web/app/globals.css (#742 state tokens + @theme exports the chip consumes), web/components/inspector/family_segregation_badge.tsx (existing badge pattern), web/components/ui/badge.tsx (shadcn badge if present).

## Review focus
1. Is the 7-verdict vocabulary correct + complete vs §-1.1 (VERIFIED/PARTIAL/UNSUPPORTED/FABRICATED/UNREACHABLE) + contradiction/refusal? Any verdict missing/wrong?
2. Are PARTIAL vs contradiction (both amber) disambiguated by icon+label so they're not color-only-distinguished?
3. AA contrast of each label on its tint; icon a11y (aria-hidden + accessible label).
4. Any euphemism that softens a serious verdict (FABRICATED). Any P0/P1.

## Output schema
```yaml
verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES
novel_p0: [...]
continuing_p0: [...]
p1: [...]
p2: [...]
```

# This is NOT the Next.js you know

This version has breaking changes — APIs, conventions, and file structure may all differ from your training data. Read the relevant guide in `node_modules/next/dist/docs/` before writing any code. Heed deprecation notices.

<!-- BEGIN:polaris-evaluation-debug-standards-2026-05-09 -->

# STANDING EVALUATION & DEBUG STANDARDS (binding)

Per project `CLAUDE.md` §-1 (added 2026-05-09 per user directive).

## §-1.1 Line-by-line audit standard (clinical-safety-critical)

Every content/quality evaluation MUST be a **line-by-line, claim-by-claim, citation-by-citation, reasoning-step-by-reasoning-step** audit against the **actually-fetched source content** using the highest industrial benchmarks (PRISMA 2020, AMSTAR-2, GRADE per claim, ICMJE, ICH-GCP, Cochrane RoB 2 / ROBINS-I / QUADAS-2, jurisdiction-specific frameworks: FDA label, EMA SmPC, Health Canada PM, NICE TA, MHRA AR, TGA PI, PMDA review, NMPA labeling).

Both Claude AND Codex MUST run independent audits in parallel. Cross-review combines findings.

Per-claim verdict required: VERIFIED / PARTIAL / UNSUPPORTED / FABRICATED / UNREACHABLE — with the specific evidence span quote that supports the verdict.

**STRICTLY BANNED:** word counts, citation counts, unique-source counts, pattern presence ("does the report mention X?"), sample-based audits ("we audited 5 of 50 claims"), string-presence checks (PASS/FAIL on "does it cite [N]?"), metadata comparison ("ChatGPT has 27 contradictions, POLARIS has 3, so ChatGPT is better"), spot-checking, "looks coherent" assertions without reading every cited span.

**Why:** clinical context. Pattern-matching evaluation will miss real fabrications. Patients can be hurt by a wrong dose, wrong contraindication, wrong indication population that survived a metadata check. Length is liability, not advantage.

## §-1.2 Standard debug workflow (no §3.0 bypass)

Every task / bug / issue follows this sequence (§-1.2 governs _task-work_; the §3.1 boot ritual and §3.0 halt-marker checks always run first and can preempt this sequence):

1. **GitHub Issue FIRST** (first _task-work_ call). `gh issue create` BEFORE any branch, code, or brief. Title `I-<prefix>-NNN — <one-line summary>`; body has acceptance criteria.
2. **Comprehensive grep/scan adjacent files.** Before writing the brief, grep all call sites, consumers, downstream rule checks, tests. List them in the brief under "Files I have ALSO checked and they're clean: [...]".
3. **Smoke test offline** (single sentence/section), NOT a full sweep, before claiming the change works.
4. **Codex brief** with the iter-1 cap directive verbatim + adjacent-file scan results so Codex VERIFIES rather than discovers.
5. **Codex APPROVE → diff → Codex APPROVE on diff.** Goal: 1-2 iters per task, not 5. The 5-cap is a backstop, not a target.
6. **If a big bug surfaces and cannot be resolved in 5 iters, mark URGENT new GitHub Issue and resolve it FIRST.** Do not let cap-5 force-approve a real production blocker.
7. **Close the GitHub Issue when the PR merges.** Do not leave resolved issues open.

<!-- END:polaris-evaluation-debug-standards-2026-05-09 -->

<!-- BEGIN:polaris-restart-2026-05-05 -->

# POLARIS issue-driven workflow (mandatory)

Per project `CLAUDE.md` §3.0 + `state/polaris_restart/plan.md`:

- Every unit of work is a GitHub Issue from `state/polaris_restart/issue_breakdown.md`.
- Per-Issue 5-artifact triple required (brief + verdict + diff + audit + claude_audit). CI enforcement lands at PR-D; pre-PR-D, Codex review enforces.
- Per plan §7.A LOCKED A2 + §7.B LOCKED B1: Claude writes briefs + diffs; Codex reviews twice per Issue (brief + diff); CI required check `polaris/codex-required` gates GitHub auto-merge; user reads `git log` morning.
- **HARD ITERATION CAP: 5 per Codex review** (added 2026-05-06 per CLAUDE.md §8.3.1). If Codex has not APPROVE'd by iter 5, Claude force-APPROVE's and ships, capturing residual concerns as follow-up Issues. Every brief MUST include the verbatim cap directive (see CLAUDE.md §8.3.3 + `.codex/REVIEW_BRIEF_FORMAT.md` §0).
- **Resource discipline (CPU/RAM/GPU)** per CLAUDE.md §8.4 (added 2026-05-06): one `codex exec` at a time; kill leftover python/node/codex processes between iters; no heavy ML/CUDA processes in autonomous loops; track long-running dev servers and kill before next Issue; pre/post-task `Get-Process` inventory.
- `gh pr merge --admin` REVOKED from Claude.

Read project `CLAUDE.md` §3.0 + §10 boot ritual before any frontend work.

// I-cd-013a (GH#609) — Two-family invariant badge.
// Source of truth: verified_report.evaluator_model + verified_report.family_segregation_passed
// (NOT metadata.json — corrected at brief iter 1 P1).
import type { VerifiedReportShape } from "@/lib/inspector_bundle_loader";
import type { BundleManifest } from "@/lib/signed_bundle";

interface FamilySegregationBadgeProps {
  manifest: BundleManifest;
  verifiedReport: VerifiedReportShape;
}

export function FamilySegregationBadge({
  manifest,
  verifiedReport,
}: FamilySegregationBadgeProps) {
  const passed = verifiedReport.family_segregation_passed;
  return (
    <div
      data-testid="family-segregation-badge"
      data-state={passed ? "pass" : "fail"}
      className="border-border bg-card flex flex-col gap-2 rounded-md border p-4 text-sm"
    >
      <div className="flex items-center justify-between gap-3">
        <p className="font-medium">Two-family invariant</p>
        <span
          className={
            passed
              ? "inline-flex items-center rounded-md bg-emerald-100 px-2 py-0.5 text-xs font-medium text-emerald-900 dark:bg-emerald-950 dark:text-emerald-200"
              : "inline-flex items-center rounded-md bg-rose-100 px-2 py-0.5 text-xs font-medium text-rose-900 dark:bg-rose-950 dark:text-rose-200"
          }
        >
          {passed ? "Pass" : "Fail"}
        </span>
      </div>
      <div className="grid grid-cols-1 gap-2 sm:grid-cols-2">
        <div>
          <p className="text-muted-foreground text-xs tracking-wide uppercase">
            Generator
          </p>
          <p className="font-mono text-sm">{manifest.generator_model}</p>
        </div>
        <div>
          <p className="text-muted-foreground text-xs tracking-wide uppercase">
            Evaluator
          </p>
          <p className="font-mono text-sm">{verifiedReport.evaluator_model}</p>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}

# Codex BRIEF review — I-p2-005 (#744): per-claim verdict chip

HARD ITERATION CAP: 5 per document. This is iter 1 of 5.
- Front-load ALL real findings in iter 1. No drip-feeding.
- Same quality bar regardless of iteration count.
- "Don't pick bone from egg" — reserve P0/P1 for real execution risks; cosmetics → P2/P3.
- If iter 5 returns REQUEST_CHANGES, force-APPROVE on non-P0/P1; do not bank for iter 6.
- Surface held-back findings now.
- Verdict APPROVE iff zero NOVEL P0 AND zero continuing P0 AND zero P1.

## Task
A reusable **per-claim verdict chip** (TIER 2; report/audit pages reuse it) showing a claim's verification verdict with a state color + icon + label (NOT color-only).

## Verified current state (grounded)
- NO per-claim verdict enum exists in the frontend yet (grep: only ScopeVerdict = accepted|needs_clarification|rejected, which is the SCOPE gate, NOT per-claim). So #744 INTRODUCES the canonical verdict vocabulary as a presentation primitive; the data layer feeds it (report page #756 / audit #759 wire real values).
- #742 state tokens exist + @theme-exported: --verified (green), --contradiction (amber), --refusal (neutral), --destructive (maroon), each with -foreground; plus --muted for neutral.
- Canonical §-1.1 audit vocabulary: VERIFIED / PARTIAL / UNSUPPORTED / FABRICATED / UNREACHABLE (+ contradiction, refusal as run states).

## Acceptance criteria (diff implements; brief reviews the plan)
1. `web/components/verdict/verdict_chip.tsx`: prop `verdict: VerdictKind` (typed union of the 7 values). Renders a small pill: state-color tint + an icon/glyph + the label text (THREE signals — never color alone, per rubric a11y).
2. Token mapping (distinct + AA): VERIFIED→--verified(green,✓); PARTIAL→amber(~, caution); UNSUPPORTED→--muted/neutral(—, no evidence); FABRICATED→--destructive(maroon,✕, serious); UNREACHABLE→muted dashed(?, not fetched); contradiction→--contradiction(amber,⚠ conflict); refusal→--refusal(neutral, shield). PARTIAL vs contradiction both amber-family MUST be disambiguated by icon+label (color not sole signal).
3. Honest semantics: each label is accurate (FABRICATED is a real, serious verdict; UNREACHABLE ≠ UNSUPPORTED). No euphemism.
4. Frontier-Minimal; WCAG 2.2 (AA contrast on each tint, icon has aria-hidden + the label is the accessible name, target sizing if interactive — chip is non-interactive display).

## Files I have ALSO checked and they're clean
- web/app/globals.css (#742 state tokens + @theme exports the chip consumes), web/components/inspector/family_segregation_badge.tsx (existing badge pattern), web/components/ui/badge.tsx (shadcn badge if present).

## Review focus
1. Is the 7-verdict vocabulary correct + complete vs §-1.1 (VERIFIED/PARTIAL/UNSUPPORTED/FABRICATED/UNREACHABLE) + contradiction/refusal? Any verdict missing/wrong?
2. Are PARTIAL vs contradiction (both amber) disambiguated by icon+label so they're not color-only-distinguished?
3. AA contrast of each label on its tint; icon a11y (aria-hidden + accessible label).
4. Any euphemism that softens a serious verdict (FABRICATED). Any P0/P1.

## Output schema
```yaml
verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES
novel_p0: [...]
continuing_p0: [...]
p1: [...]
p2: [...]
```

outputs/audits\continuous\4f20140_audit.md:22:- **C-sec-form-button-default-type (probe 2):** PASS. The new template `<button role="radio">` at `dashboard/page.tsx:233-251` carries explicit `type="button"`. **This is critical** — without `type="button"`, a `<button>` inside `<form onSubmit>` defaults to `type="submit"`, which would mean keyboard activation (Space) of a radio fires `onSubmit` prematurely. The dashboard's `onSubmit` runs `createRun(...)` if scope wasn't rejected — premature submit on first Space-press would bypass the scope check the user is meant to consciously trigger. The author got this right. The same `type="button"` discipline holds on the F-25-modified `remove` button (`:333`), the EvidenceTooltip Trigger button (`:33`), and the contradiction pill (`inspector/[runId]/page.tsx:297`).

state\codex_consultations\plan_v7_codex_response.md:1486:outputs\polaris_graph\GEMINI_E2E_20260316_092353_report.md:1:# Comparative Analysis of Adhesion Testing Methods for Polymer Coatings: Methodologies, Performance, and Regulatory Compliance ## Abstract This research report presents a comparative analysis of adhesion testing methods for polymer coatings, specifically evaluating pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. Spanning 15 sections and comprising 16,701 words, the study examines standardized test method definitions within ASTM and ISO frameworks alongside material suitability limits and thickness constraints. The methodology involves a rigorous review of ASTM D4541 protocols for pull-off testing and ASTM D903 and ISO11339 compliance for peel test variations, including 90-degree and T-peel configurations. Furthermore, the analysis addresses knife and scrape test methods for hard coatings, surface contaminants, and adhesion performance scoring scales. Key findings highlight the impact of environmental temperature and immersion effects on adhesion, alongside theoretical adhesion modeling and cohesive failure analysis. The report classifies failure modes and mechanism units while assessing urethane adhesive capabilities and bond strength applications. Administrative testing documentation and warranty requirements are also scrutinized to ensure regulatory compliance. Drawing upon 84 total unique sources cited and incorporating 218 total citations in report, this work establishes critical guidelines for selecting appropriate testing methodologies. The implications suggest that strict adherence to standardized protocols enhances reliability in predicting coating performance under varying environmental conditions, thereby informing industry best practices for quality assurance and warranty validation in polymer coating applications. ## Standardized Test Method Definitions and ASTM ISO Frameworks Adhesion testing frameworks for polymer coatings are governed by a complex array of ASTM and ISO standards that define methodological rigor and compliance thresholds [1]. Additionally, these standardized protocols establish the baseline for comparing pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials [2]. Furthermore, the regulatory landscape distinguishes between quantitative tensile measurements and qualitative visual assessments, each serving specific diagnostic purposes within the coating lifecycle [3]. Pull-off adhesion testing applies tensile force perpendicular to the substrate to measure coating resistance to separation [1]. This method is utilized to gage adhesive strength of the coating once applied and cured as per manufacturer's guidelines [2]. Moreover, the pull-off adhesion tester measures adhesion of coatings to metal, wood, concrete, and rigid substrates, providing versatility across material classes [4]. Pull-off test data provides quantitative indication of bond tensile strength [5]. In addition, industrial-grade adhesives typically have tensile strength ranging from 10 MPa to over 60 MPa depending on formulation [6]. Hydroxyapatite coatings showed repeatable adhesion strength of 3.4 ± 1.0 MPa across both ASTM test methods [7]. ASTM C633-01 test uses the reported value diameter cylindrical blocks with 38 mm length for adhesion testing. Moreover, ASTM D4541 and BS EN ISO 4624 describe pull-off adhesion testing standards for protective coatings [8]. The pull-off test is standardized by ISO 4624:2023 and is widely used to quantify adhesion [9]. ASTM D7234 defines the standard test method for pull-off adhesion strength on concrete using portable testers [10]. Pull-off testing follows ISO 4624, ASTM D4541, and D7234 standards for evaluating coating peel strength [11]. Valid pull-off test requires coating to cover at least 50% of stamp surface after detachment [11]. In addition, the Cross-Cut Test provides qualitative evaluation while the Pull-Off Method provides quantitative evaluation of coating adhesion [3]. This distinction is critical when selecting methods for epoxy, urethane, or silicone systems, as quantitative data may be required for structural warranty validation. Additionally, cross-Cut Test classifies adhesion results into six levels according to the degree of peeling after tape removal [3]. The procedure involves making lattice-pattern cuts through the coating to the substrate, applying adhesive tape, and peeling in one motion [3]. ASTM D3359 outlines standardized test methods for evaluating adhesion of coatings to metallic substrates using pressure-sensitive tape [12]. This standard is widely recognized for evaluating coating adhesion on metal substrates using the tape test method [13]. ISO 2409 specifies the cross-cut test method for evaluating adhesion of coatings on sheet metal parts [13]. ASTM D3359 defines adhesion as molecular attraction and mechanical bonds between coating and substrate [14]. ASTM D3359 procedures assess the adhesion of coating films specifically on metallic substrates [15]. The tape test involves applying and removing pressure-sensitive tape over cuts made in the film [15]. The tape test is qualitative and cannot precisely assess quantitatively the actual strength of the adhesion [16]. Cross-Cut Test uses pressure-sensitive tape spread over cuts and rapidly pulled off [17]. Adhesion is rated by inspecting the cut area for coating removal relative to classifications 0B through 5B [16]. Cross-cut adhesion test evaluation ranges from 0B representing complete detachment to 5B representing no detachment [18]. ASTM D3359 Standard Test Method for Measuring Adhesion by Tape Test was codified in 1974 [19]. ASTM D3359 is a standard test method for measuring adhesion by tape test [20]. Crosshatch adhesion testing follows ASTM D3359 standard with grid cuts through coating [21]. ASTM D3359 is the standard method for rating adhesion by tape test [22]. Peel testing represents a third category, measuring the force required to strip a flexible coating from a substrate [23]. Peel tests measure adhesive strength by pulling one material away from another at a specific angle and speed [24]. This method is suitable for designs where bonded edges experience prying or bending [25]. Peel and adhesion strength testing measures bond strength when force attempts separation [25]. Comprehensive adhesive suitability requires comparing peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance [26]. The peel test is a method used in materials science to measure the bond strength between two materials [27]. Peel strength is usually reported in units of force per width such as N/mm or lb/in [27]. Peel testing methods include T-peel and 90-degree peel tests standardized under ASTM D1876 and D6862 [28]. Standard testing procedures evaluate peel force under constant temperature 23±1°C and 50±5% RH [29]. Peel test values represent an average between 5 and the reported value of peel for each of two tests. T-peel testing was performed according to BS 5350-C12:1994/ISO 11339:1993 standard on aluminium alloy [30]. Chinese national standard GB/T 2790-1995 specifies the 180° peel strength test method steps and requirements [31]. ISO 8510-2 covers 180-degree peel test for flexible-bonded-to-rigid test specimen assembly [24]. The T shaped peel test ASTM D1876 is most widely used for thin-gauge metal adherends [26]. Table 1 summarizes the comparative frameworks for these primary adhesion testing methods, highlighting standard designations and operational limits. | Test Method | Primary Standard (ASTM/ISO) | Substrate Suitability | Measurement Type | Thickness Limit | |:--- |:--- |:--- |:--- |:--- | | Pull-Off | ASTM D4541, ISO 4624 | Metal, Concrete, Rigid | Quantitative (MPa) | N/A [11] | | Cross-Cut | ASTM D3359, ISO 2409 | Metal, Plastic, Rigid | Qualitative (0B-5B) | 250 µm [32] | | Peel | ASTM D1876, ISO 11339 | Flexible-to-Rigid | Quantitative (N/mm) | N/A [28] | | Knife/Scrape | ASTM D6677 | Hard Coatings | Qualitative/Force | N/A [22] | Thickness constraints significantly influence method selection for polymer coatings. The Cross-Cut Test is limited by coating thickness with a maximum of 250 micrometers [32]. Cross-cut test is applicable for layer thicknesses up to about 250 µm [33]. This limitation renders the cross-cut method unsuitable for thick urethane or epoxy linings often exceeding this threshold. The test method covers determination of practical adhesion strength of hard ceramic coatings on metal and ceramic substrates [34]. Test method is commonly used for coating thicknesses in the range of 0.1 to 30 micrometers [34]. Adhesion pull-off strength testing determines the overall quality and integrity of an installed coating system [35]. Cross hatch tape testing is intended for laboratory use on coatings under 5 mils thickness [22]. Scrape test adhesion is measured in kilograms of force required for removal [36]. ASTM D6677 is the standard method for evaluating adhesion by knife [22]. Cross-cut test is especially suitable for thin layers on rigid substrates like metal or plastic [33]. The crosscut adhesion test is a standard ASTM or ISO procedure for dry coatings [37]. Conflicting evidence exists regarding the fundamental capabilities of these tests and specific procedural dimensions. One source states the pull-off adhesion test measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface [4]. However, conflicting evidence indicates the test method does not measure fundamental adhesion strength of the bond between coating and substrate [34]. This discrepancy suggests a distinction between practical field adhesion and theoretical interfacial strength. Regarding cut patterns, evidence states cross-cut test uses a tool to cut a right-angle lattice pattern through the coating to the substrate [1]. Other evidence indicates X-cut tape test uses a sharp tool to make an X-shaped cut through the coating to substrate [4]. ASTM D3359 accommodates both via Method A (X-Cut) and Method B (Cross-Cut Tape Test), with Method B preferred for laboratory or controlled environments [12]. Test Method A (X-Cut Tape Test) is designed primarily for field use with X-shaped cut in coating. Cross-Hatch Test is typically used on witness samples of the same substrate material [38]. Procedural variations also appear in peel test specimen dimensions. Evidence indicates DOWSIL ALLGUARD coating adhesion test uses cheesecloth strip 1 inch by 12 inches embedded in wet coating [39]. In conflict, another source states peel test uses 1 inch wide by 6 inch long fabric strip embedded in coating base layer [40]. These variations likely reflect specific manufacturer protocols versus general standardized methods. ISO 4624 is referenced as a global standard for pull-off adhesion testing compliance [5]. Traditional adhesion tests like ISO/JIS cross-hatch and ASTM tape tests only evaluate final adhesion strength without explaining adhesion mechanisms [41]. Surface free energy and adhesion parameter measurements are proposed as complementary methods to traditional cross-cut tape tests [41]. Adhesion testing mechanics are complicated when polymer adhesive and metal substrate have different elastic moduli [42]. This complexity is particularly relevant for silicone and urethane coatings which exhibit different elastic properties compared to epoxy systems. Peel and pull-off data should not be compared since testing mechanisms differ [14]. Four common methods exist for testing adhesion of protective coatings and linings per ASTM standards [14]. Common adhesion measuring techniques are performed using a knife or a pull-off adhesion tester. Specific material considerations emerge within the evidence regarding epoxy and silicone systems. Results showed good agreement between hot water-adhesion tests and TxDOT peel tests [43]. This suggests environmental conditioning impacts epoxy coating performance assessment. The majority of bond systems underwent tensile adhesion strength testing using a button-to-plate configuration [44]. Silicone contamination impacts bond performance, necessitating rigorous testing protocols [44]. Video How to Perform an Adhesion Test Patch provides more information on roof coating adhesion tests [45]. This indicates field verification is critical for urethane and silicone roof applications. Adhesion test must be conducted prior to estimating coating restoration project per Inland adhesion testing procedures [46]. Adhesive performance evaluation after testing includes checking bonding strength, color stability, surface integrity, and chemical stability [47]. Tensile or shear tests measure strength between bonded materials for adhesives [48]. The study objective was to identify an optimal adhesive for the pull-off test providing higher adhesion [9]. Pull-off method provides values for peel stress allowing quick determination and comparison of coating adhesion [11]. The proposed adhesion testing method uses direct pull-off strength measurements at various corrosion stages [49]. Field adhesion test recommended when laboratory test cannot be performed on-site [50]. Field adhesion test also called hand pull test verifies proper sealant installation. There is no universally accepted test for conformal coating adhesion [38]. SAE J400 standard outlines specialized coating adhesion test method using Instron testing machine [13]. Latter two ASTM methods evaluate tensile or pull-off strength of coating [14]. The test method was originally developed for ductile coatings applied to metal substrates [16]. Site adhesion testing must be verified according to criteria on page 35 to confirm proper surface preparation [51]. Nitto Denko employs adhesive pull strength evaluation using JIS Z0237 and ASTM standards [52]. Mechanical analysis services include testing for adhesive strength, tensile strength, yield stress. All structural applications must be tested by the Dow test lab for surface preparation recommendations. A similar testing program is needed for concrete coatings to enable laboratory verification. Adhesive testing products include epoxy glue, polyurethane glue, and organic silicone among many others [47]. Tensile bulk tests were performed using long dogbone specimens to characterize adhesives at room temperature [53]. Higher yield strength values obtained in compression test than in tensile test for all adhesives. Study of size dependence of bulk specimen was carried out to evaluate scale factors in adhesive testing. NPL and partners developed a tape peel test applicable to PCB or component surfaces [54]. ATS offers ASTM paint adhesion testing to determine adhesive performance according to industry standards. Peel tests involve pulling flexible material from a surface at defined angles [48]. **Key Findings** * Pull-off methods (ASTM D4541, ISO 4624) provide quantitative tensile strength data suitable for thick epoxy and urethane systems, whereas cross-cut tests (ASTM D3359) offer qualitative ratings limited to coatings under 250 µm [32]. * Conflicting evidence exists regarding whether pull-off tests measure fundamental adhesion strength or practical bond integrity, indicating a need for method selection based on specific performance criteria [4][34]. * Peel testing standards (ASTM D1876, ISO 11339) quantify force per width (N/mm) for flexible-to-rigid bonds, essential for silicone and urethane membrane evaluations [28][27]. * ASTM D3359 encompasses both X-cut and lattice patterns, with the lattice method preferred for laboratory precision and the X-cut for field applications [1]. * Environmental conditions and substrate elastic moduli significantly influence test outcomes, requiring controlled temperature and humidity during peel and tensile assessments [42][29]. ! Adhesion strength of coating to substrate defines overall coating integrity and performance [55]. Significantly, the applicability of specific adhesion testing methods varies significantly based on the physical properties of the coating material and the thickness of the applied layer. Crosshatch testing is most suitable for thin coatings on rigid substrates [21]. Thickness constraints serve as a primary determinant for method selection across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. The evidence indicates distinct operational ranges for common testing methodologies. Top coat thickness ranged from 28 to 638 micrometers in the duplex coating system [56]. Cured adhesive thicknesses were measured at 2.0 plus or minus 0.1 mils for testing [57]. | Test Method / Material Context | Thickness Constraint / Measurement | Unit | Source ID |. | Cross-Cut Test (General) | Maximum 250 | micrometers | |. | Cross-Cut Test (Laboratory) | Under 5 | mils | |. | ASTM C1624 (Hard Ceramic) | 0.1 to 30 | micrometers | |. | Duplex Coating System | 28 to 638 | micrometers | [56] |. | Cured Adhesive Specimen | 2.0 plus or minus 0.1 | mils | [57] |. For coatings up to 2.0 mils thickness, cuts are spaced the reported value apart with eleven cuts. Exceeding these thresholds compromises the lattice pattern integrity required for evaluation. Force measured in MPa or psi reveals the coating's tensile pull-off strength indicating adhesion [36]. Most concrete coating adhesion pull-off strength testing exhibits substrate failure in the 400-500 psi range [35]. The adhesion pull-off strength of the polyaspartic published in its data sheet is 900 psi [35]. Material suitability extends beyond thickness to include chemical composition and mechanical stiffness. Polyurethane coatings can possess higher stiffness than the adhesive used to fix the testing dolly in place [58]. Polyurethane coatings offer super flexibility, abrasion resistance, and UV stability for outdoor applications [5]. Increasing hard segment content in waterborne polyurethane formulation usually increases peel strength [31]. Epoxy coatings must conform to MPI ASM No. 116 with polyamide-cured epoxy-resin material for finish [59]. Epoxy bonded to silicone duplex coating pull-off force thickness dependence agreed with modified Kendall model for elastomeric coatings [56]. Category 4 silicone-resin coatings must contain minimum the reported value silicone resin and maximum 49 percent. Methodological distinctions between qualitative and quantitative evaluation impact data reliability for these materials. Peel strength is calculated using force applied during peeling process divided by film width. This limitation suggests that while pull-off testing measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface, it may not isolate interfacial chemistry. Conflicting evidence exists regarding specific procedural dimensions and measurement capabilities. This discrepancy indicates variability in specimen preparation standards across different manufacturers and material types. Surface prep chemistry shapes coating outcomes ranging from adhesion to corrosion resistance and durability [60]. Failure to verify and document adhesion results may result in adhesion loss not covered by Dow Warranty [51]. Tensile modulus was measured for three polyurethane coatings, an epoxy adhesive, and a cyanoacrylate adhesive [58]. Significantly, differences in Young's modulus between test methods and manufacturer data reached up to 70% for methods [61]. Adhesive bonds can be stronger than the materials being bonded together in some automotive applications. Tungsten carbide coating adhesion exceeded epoxy adhesive strength, limiting measurement to 95 MPa maximum [7]. Cohesive strength decreases as coating undergoes weathering and pull-off strength monitors breakdown [62]. The electronics industry has demanding applications with specific adhesive performance requirements. Adhesives and potting compounds are used for die attach, glob top encapsulation, underfill, edge bonding [63]. Market expectations for removable adhesives specify 1.75 pli peel strength after 3 weeks at 70C [57]. Key peel test metrics include initial peak force, average force across entire seal, and peel strength [24]. 3M 681 tape was found to most closely reflect desired properties and performance for adhesion testing [19]. Cross-cut testing measures coating resistance to separation by cutting a right-angle lattice pattern to the substrate [23]. Acceptable adhesion shows cohesive tear within sealant or elongation beyond movement capability [50]. Mode of failure and pull-off pressure confirm proper surface preparation and coating application [2]. Failure mode for BTSE/GPS coated surface is cohesive failure when specimen is dry with fracture surface [64]. Pull-off strength important parameter when selecting polymer coating systems [62]. Coating application quality varies by coater and impacts adhesion [43]. Two coats necessary to achieve 10-mil minimum dry film thickness for coating [39]. Technology assessment analyzed 94 individual coatings and 44 coating systems for application and durability [65]. First-cost comparisons rank acrylic least expensive, silicone close behind, polyurethane higher [66]. Polyurethane coatings provide high abrasion resistance, good flexibility, chemical resistance, and UV stability [59]. * Cross-cut testing is constrained to thinner coatings, with maximum limits identified at 250 micrometers or under 5 mils, whereas pull-off methods accommodate thicker duplex systems ranging up to 638 micrometers [56]. * Polyurethane and silicone materials require specific consideration of stiffness and resin content, as polyurethane coatings can exceed the stiffness of testing adhesives and silicone-resin coatings mandate minimum 22 percent resin composition [58]. * Method selection dictates data type, with cross-cut tests providing qualitative evaluation via lattice patterns and pull-off tests delivering quantitative tensile strength measurements in MPa or psi [36]. * Procedural conflicts exist in specimen preparation, evidenced by divergent peel test strip dimensions of 1x6 inches versus 1x12 inches depending on the specific coating manufacturer standards [40][39]. Specifically, *Figure 2: Bar chart displaying document engagement metrics such as views and ratings. | Published Year | 2019 | year |. *Table 1: Key Timeline Events for ASTM C1624*. ## Pull-Off Testing Mechanical Application and Procedures Pull-off testing represents a primary quantitative methodology for evaluating bond strength, distinct from qualitative assessments found in cross-cut or peel configurations. This perpendicular application differentiates pull-off mechanics from peel tests, which measure the force required to strip a flexible coating from a substrate at specific angles [23]. In particular, for polymer coatings such as epoxy, urethane, and silicone, the choice of mechanical application directly influences the interpretability of adhesion strength regarding regulatory compliance and field performance. The core mechanism utilizes a fixture, often referred to as a dolly, which is adhesively bonded to the cured coating surface [36]. Once bonded, the tester exerts a pulling force until failure occurs, providing a quantitative indication of bond tensile strength. This mechanical setup allows for the direct determination of adhesion strength at the metallisation or polymer interface from the substrate [67]. The mechanical distinction is critical for the research question comparing methods across materials; pull-off tests are typical for industrial coatings including polymer coatings and epoxy resins, whereas cross-cut tests are especially suitable for thin layers on rigid substrates like metal or plastic. Standardization governs the mechanical procedures to ensure comparability across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. The evolution of these standards reflects the industry's shift toward quantitative evaluation methods. The following table outlines key standards and their establishment or revision dates relevant to mechanical adhesion testing procedures. | Standard Designation | Method Type | Establishment/Revision Year | Evaluation Type |. | ASTM D3359 | Tape Test | 1974 [19] | Qualitative |. | JIS K 5600-5-6 | Cross-Cut Test | 1999 | Qualitative |. | JIS K 5600-5-7 | Pull-Off Method | 2014 | Quantitative |. | ISO 4624 | Pull-Off Adhesion | 2023 [9] | Quantitative [9] |. The temporal progression in the table indicates a standardization trend toward quantitative tensile testing methods like ISO 4624 and JIS K 5600-5-7. However, conflicting evidence exists regarding the specific scope and dates of Japanese Industrial Standards. One source specifies JIS K 5600-5-7: 2014 for the Pull-Off Method as a coating tensile adhesion test for quantitative evaluation. Another source indicates JIS K 5600-5-6: 1999 specifies the Cross-Cut Test method for coating adhesion evaluation under Japanese Industrial Standards. While the conflict detector flags this as a contradiction, the evidence suggests these represent different parts of the same standard framework addressing different mechanical methods rather than mutually exclusive facts. The 1999 date applies to cross-cut mechanics, while the 2014 date applies to pull-off mechanics, highlighting the divergent procedural requirements within the same regulatory body. Similarly, distinctions in ASTM standards clarify the mechanical application differences. These standards support different mechanical actions; the tape test relies on adhesive removal force, while the knife test evaluates resistance to scraping. Quantitative outcomes from pull-off mechanics offer specific metrics for epoxy and silicone materials. Specific material data shows a button pull-off test measured exterior paint average bond strength at 7.27 MPa (1050 psi) [68]. These numerical values underscore the quantitative advantage of pull-off mechanics over the 0B to 5B classification range found in cross-cut adhesion test evaluation [18]. For silicone rubber and epoxy resin interfaces, pull-off tests were applied to estimate adhesion effect, demonstrating utility across these specific polymer types [69]. Mechanical application procedures also dictate sample validity. This requirement ensures that the measured force reflects coating adhesion rather than adhesive failure between the dolly and the coating. This mechanical difference implies that peel tests may better simulate edge failures in flexible silicone or urethane coatings, while pull-off tests better simulate normal stress failures in rigid epoxy systems. Environmental and procedural factors further influence mechanical test outcomes. These factors are critical when comparing methods across materials, as urethane and silicone adhesives may cure differently under varying conditions. In particular, relying solely on one mechanical method, such as the cross-cut test which uses pressure-sensitive tape spread over cuts and rapidly pulled off, may not capture the full performance profile of complex polymer systems [17]. Field application mechanics differ from laboratory settings. A simple qualitative pull test can be performed by hand by pulling silicone with reasonable strength. Nevertheless, this mismatch affects stress distribution during pull-off or peel procedures. Regulatory compliance often dictates the mechanical method selected. Note that while these standards evaluate peel strength, the mechanical action is tensile pull-off, distinguishing it from flexible peel testing defined by ISO 8510-2 which covers 180-degree peel test for flexible-bonded-to-rigid test specimen assembly. These distinctions are vital for the research question, as epoxy coatings typically require tensile pull-off (ASTM D4541), while flexible silicone or urethane membranes may require peel configurations (ASTM D1876). Cross-cut test classification based on percentage of area affected by coating removal provides a visual metric distinct from force measurements [68]. This prohibition underscores the necessity of method-specific benchmarks when comparing epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. * Pull-off testing applies perpendicular tensile force via a dolly to provide quantitative adhesion strength values in MPa or psi, distinguishing it from qualitative cross-cut methods. * Standardization trends show a progression from qualitative tape tests (ASTM D3359, 1974) to quantitative pull-off methods (ISO 4624, 2023), influencing regulatory compliance for polymer coatings. * Mechanical suitability varies by material, with pull-off tests typical for epoxy resins and rigid substrates, while peel tests are suitable for flexible coatings experiencing prying or bending [25]. * Peel and pull-off data mechanisms differ significantly, preventing direct comparison of strength values across these testing methodologies. ! This method is recognized as the standard test method for pull-off strength of coatings using portable testers [15]. Indeed, the distinction between quantitative and qualitative evaluation is critical when comparing epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials, as performance metrics differ significantly across these chemistries. This divergence influences material selection, particularly for structural applications where precise bond strength data is required. For polymer coatings where tensile integrity is paramount, pull-off protocols offer superior data fidelity. | Pull-Off | ASTM D4541 | Quantitative | Tensile Force |. | Cross-Cut | ASTM D3359 | Qualitative | 0B to 5B Scale [18] |. | Cross-Cut | ISO 2409 | Qualitative | Detachment Evaluation |. This creates a distinction between general coating standards and substrate-specific protocols. While D4541 is broad, D7234 is specialized, suggesting that concrete applications require distinct validation compared to metallic substrates used for epoxy or urethane systems. The existence of multiple standards necessitates careful selection based on the substrate material to ensure regulatory compliance. A significant conflict exists regarding the fundamental nature of the data produced by these tests. This discrepancy implies that while pull-off tests provide quantitative force data, they may reflect cohesive failure within the coating or adhesive rather than pure interfacial adhesion. Therefore, while ASTM D4541 provides numerical values, these values might represent a combination of adhesive and cohesive strengths. For the research question comparing epoxy, urethane, and silicone, this means pull-off data indicates system integrity but may not isolate material-specific interfacial chemistry performance. Material suitability further differentiates these protocols. Thicker polymer systems, common in protective urethane applications, may exceed this constraint, necessitating pull-off methods. However, ASTM D3359 is used as an adhesion standard despite not being designed for conformal coatings [38]. This misapplication risk underscores the need for strict adherence to ASTM D4541 for thicker or non-metallic systems. Specific performance metrics vary by material and substrate combination. These variations highlight that absolute strength values are material-dependent. This suggests that for silicone, pull-off configurations must account for potential contamination effects on the interface. Regulatory compliance extends beyond ASTM to international standards. This contrasts with the 1999 cross-cut specification. The newer 2014 standard aligns with the quantitative trend seen in ASTM D4541, suggesting a shift toward tensile measurement in regulatory frameworks. Compliance with these varied standards requires testing laboratories to maintain equipment capable of both tensile and tape-based evaluations. ASTM D4541 Standard Test Method for Pull-Off Strength requires portable adhesion tester apparatus. The ASTM D4541 Pull-Off Test measures tensile pull-off force required to detach dolly adhesively bonded to coating from substrate. While peel tests address specific stress modes, pull-off tests address normal stress. ASTM D4541 pull-off test is a fundamental standard for measuring adhesive strength on metallic substrates [70]. For epoxy adhesives, toughened epoxy adhesive Bondmaster E3334 was tested with ATBN reactive liquid polymer toughening agent [30]. The performance of such modified epoxies under ASTM D4541 protocols would differ from unmodified systems, necessitating specific calibration. Scoring systems also vary between methods. Method A results assessed on 0A-5A scale where 5A indicates no peeling and 0A indicates complete removal. These qualitative scales contrast with the continuous numerical data from pull-off tests. The FAS-CeO2/PDMS coating achieved cross-cut adhesion classification of level 2 corresponding to 4B rating. This specific data point illustrates how qualitative ratings map to material performance. However, independent laboratories have noted differences of up to 50 percent in tape adhesion variability over time. This variability reinforces the preference for ASTM D4541 in high-stakes applications where precision is required. ASTM-D-4541 standard may be used for portable adhesion tester in laboratory or field environment [71]. This flexibility supports the use of pull-off methods for in-situ verification of epoxy, urethane, and silicone coatings after application. ASTM D4541 standard states scoring around the fixture violates the fundamental test criterion for un. Adherence to these procedural constraints is necessary to validate the quantitative data generated. * ASTM D4541 and ISO 4624 provide quantitative tensile force measurements, whereas ASTM D3359 and ISO 2409 offer qualitative scale-based evaluations. * Conflicting evidence exists on whether pull-off tests measure fundamental adhesion strength or overall system integrity, suggesting results may include cohesive failure components. * Substrate specificity is critical, with ASTM D7234 designated for concrete and ASTM D4541 for general coatings, impacting test selection for polymer systems [10]. * Cross-cut methods are limited to coatings under 5 mils thickness and rigid substrates, restricting their use for thicker urethane or silicone applications. * Variability in tape adhesion testing can reach 50 percent, supporting the use of pull-off protocols for higher precision in regulatory compliance. ## Peel Test Variations Including 90-Degree and T-Peel Configurations Peel test variations offer distinct mechanical insights for flexible polymer coatings compared to pull-off standardization protocols. # POLARIS PROJECT - Operational Directives

CRITICAL: This file (CLAUDE.md) MUST be read in its entirety at the start of EVERY session and immediately following ANY restart. Failure to adhere to these directives constitutes a critical operational failure. See §3 for the mandatory startup protocol.

Document Class: Operational, Binding, Project-Specific
Scope: All POLARIS development, research pipeline implementation, and maintenance tasks.

---

## §-1. STANDING EVALUATION & DEBUG STANDARDS (binding for ALL work)

**These two standards override every other directive in this file when they conflict. Both flagged by user 2026-05-09 night as repeat-violation patterns.**

### §-1.1 Line-by-line audit standard (clinical-safety-critical)

Every evaluation, audit, comparison, benchmark, regression check, BEAT-BOTH framing, or quality assessment **MUST** be:

1. **Claim-by-claim** against the actually-fetched source content (cited span text, not title or abstract).
2. **Reasoning-step-by-reasoning-step** — verify each piece of reasoning follows from the cited evidence.
3. **Citation-by-citation** — verify each citation is appropriate for the claim.
4. **Apply industrial benchmarks** appropriate to the domain:
   - Clinical: PRISMA 2020, AMSTAR-2, GRADE per claim, ICMJE authorship/COI, ICH-GCP for trial methods, Cochrane RoB 2 / ROBINS-I / QUADAS-2.
   - Regulatory: jurisdiction-specific (FDA label, EMA SmPC, Health Canada PM, NICE TA, MHRA AR, TGA PI, PMDA review, NMPA labeling).
   - Methodology: appropriate risk-of-bias tool for the study type.
5. **Both Claude and Codex run independent line-by-line audits in parallel.** Cross-review combines findings.
6. Per-claim verdict: **VERIFIED / PARTIAL / UNSUPPORTED / FABRICATED / UNREACHABLE** with the specific cited span quote that supports the verdict.

**STRICTLY BANNED:**
- Word counts / citation counts / unique-source counts as quality signals
- Pattern presence ("does the report mention 'tirzepatide'?")
- Sample-based audits ("we audited 5 of 50 claims")
- String-presence PASS/FAIL checks (e.g., gate tables)
- Metadata comparison (e.g., "ChatGPT has 27 contradictions, POLARIS has 3, so ChatGPT is better" — this framing is **lethal in clinical context**)

**Why this matters:** clinical context. Pattern-matching evaluation will miss real fabrications. Patients can be hurt by a wrong dose, wrong contraindication, wrong indication population that survived a metadata check. "It is lethal" is literal.

**Application:** when the user asks for an audit/evaluation/comparison/benchmark, line-by-line is the ONLY acceptable answer. If you propose a metadata or pattern audit, you have failed the user.

### §-1.2 Standard debug workflow (no §3.0 bypass)

Every task / bug / issue follows this exact sequence — no shortcuts:

1. **GitHub Issue FIRST.** `gh issue create` BEFORE any branch, any code, any brief. Title format: `I-<prefix>-NNN — <summary>`. Body has acceptance criteria. **No exceptions.**
2. **Comprehensive grep/scan adjacent files.** Before writing the brief, grep all call sites, consumers, downstream rule checks, and tests touching the area. List them in the brief under **"Files I have ALSO checked and they're clean: [...]"**.
3. **Smoke test offline.** Unit tests run; if the change touches the pipeline, run a minimal smoke test (single sentence, single section) BEFORE launching a full sweep. Full sweeps are NOT smoke tests.
